data.title: Virtus Newfleet Multi-Sector Short Term Bond Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Fixed Income Market Review

The third quarter of 2025 was marked by shifts in macroeconomic, geopolitical, fundamental, and supply and demand dynamics. Markets rebounded from the lows following the “Liberation Day” announcements as immediate economic impacts remained subdued. The final outcomes for tariffs and other policies remain unknown, as do their longer-term economic impacts. However, financial markets gained confidence over the quarter that the economy remained solid, inflation remained rangebound, and geopolitical risks showed early signs of easing.

Economic data in the U.S. continued to point to a resilient economy. Growth improved after a weak first half, with inflation staying contained and unemployment holding near historically low levels despite some downside surprises. This allowed the Federal Reserve (Fed) to resume easing policy with a 25 basis points (bps) reduction at the September Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) meeting. The Fed has acknowledged that while elements of its mandate are in conflict, it has chosen to focus on protecting the labor market in the near term. Global monetary policy remains desynchronized, with divergences among the European Central Bank, the Bank of England, and the Bank of Japan. Geopolitical developments were mixed during the third quarter, with volatility re-emerging in the Russia/Ukraine conflict following the Alaska summit in August, while the Middle East saw some signs of progress.

The continuing market rebound resulted in generally positive financial market performance in the quarter. Fixed income sector performance was also positive, with interest rates lower during the period. Most spread sectors outperformed U.S. Treasuries, and the U.S. Treasury curve steepened slightly. The 2-year Treasury yield decreased by 11 bps, the 5-year Treasury yield decreased by 6 bps, the 10-year Treasury yield decreased by 8 bps, and the 30-year Treasury yield moved 4 bps lower.

Fund Performance Review

The Fund (Class I) returned 1.39% in the third quarter versus the ICE BofA 1-3 Year A-BBB US Corporate Index return of 1.47%.

Positive Contributors

  • U.S. Treasury Securities: The Fund’s underweight to U.S. Treasuries had a positive impact, as most spread sectors outperformed U.S. Treasuries and had positive excess returns.
  • Emerging Markets (EM) Sovereigns: The Fund’s overweight to the EM sovereign sector was positive.
  • High Yield (HY) Corporates: Allocation to the sector was positive.
  • EM Corporates: The Fund’s allocation to EM corporates was positive.

Performance Detractors

  • Asset-Backed Securities (ABS) Selection: Issue selection versus the benchmark was negative for the period.
  • Residential Mortgage-Backed Securities (RMBS): Allocation to RMBS over agency mortgage-backed securities (MBS) and selection within RMBS had a negative impact on performance.
  • Bank Loans Selection: Issue selection and the Fund’s up-in-quality positioning were negative.

Current Fund Strategy And Positioning

  • Increased exposure to MBS, cash, EM local currency bonds, and EM sovereign bonds.
  • Reduced exposure to RMBS, investment grade (IG) corporates, bank loans, and U.S. Treasuries.
  • In addition to changes to the Fund’s sector allocation during the quarter, we maintain our efforts to optimize positioning within sectors based on our view of the best relative value.

EM Debt and Non-U.S. Exposure: The J.P. Morgan EMBI Global Diversified Index returned 4.75% in 3Q, with EM HY outperforming EM IG. EM corporate debt returned 3.18%. In 3Q, the U.S. imposed a 50% tariff on Brazilian exports, straining ties and pushing Brazil toward China. Former president Jair Bolsonaro was convicted for attempting a coup after his 2022 loss. In Argentina, President Javier Milei’s party suffered a major defeat to Peronists in Buenos Aires, sparking market volatility. Later, the U.S. pledged $20 billion in aid to support Milei’s reforms. In Ecuador, President Noboa ended diesel subsidies, triggering widespread protests as fuel prices surged. The Fund’s overall EM exposure was higher throughout the quarter. The Fund maintained a preference for U.S. dollar assets and EM HY over EM IG.

IG Corporates: IG corporate spreads ended 3Q in the low 70s—the lowest levels since 1998—while yields moved lower by 18 bps to 4.81%. Despite lower spreads and yields, IG saw positive fund flows in every week of 3Q. Institutional buyers remain active as well. Though supply for the quarter was heavier than the five-year average, it was easily absorbed. Upgrades outpaced downgrades year-to-date, though at a slower pace than in recent years. BBBs are at their lowest percentage of the overall market in a decade. We are focused on defensive industries given tight valuations, with a preference for utilities, pipelines, and capital goods.

HY Corporates: The HY market, as represented by the Bloomberg High Yield 2% Issuer Capped Bond Index, gained 2.54% in 3Q. Credit spreads tightened by 23 bps to +267 bps. Stronger-than-expected economic growth, a Fed pivot toward more monetary easing that produced a 25-bps rate cut in September, resilient corporate earnings, and significant inflows contributed to performance. Media outperformed due to anticipated deregulation and positive merger and acquisition developments. Chemicals and packaging underperformed due to weak global demand. In the Fund, we used the market rally to reduce cyclical issues, while continuing to add high-quality bonds at a discount to par.

Securitized: The securitized markets had positive total and excess returns for 3Q. Spreads tightened despite elevated issuance across all securitized sub-sectors as investors sought out additional income and spread within securitized sectors. We added agency MBS during the quarter as we felt the sector had lagged alternatives such as the IG corporate sector. While our strategy continues to focus on up-in-quality given signs of stress on the margin for certain borrowers, we’re also selectively exploring individual transactions that present promising yield opportunities lower in the capital stack.

data.title: Wall Street Breakfast Podcast: Three Forces That Defined 2025
data.description:
Listen below or on the go via Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Consumer sentiment revised lower for December as year-ahead inflation expectation revised up a tick. Tech giants brace to spend billions more in CapEx as AI race heats up. 2026 S&P 500 Outlook: I'm The Lone Bull In The China Shop.

This is an abridged transcript.

2025….has been a year.

But don’t we say the same thing at the end of every year?

Well, today we’re looking at the year as a whole and remembering three big topics that shaped 2025 and we’re peaking into 2026.

Of course, this is a countdown so we’re starting off with number three.

Vibecession…

There’s this disconnect between the average person and how they feel about the economy and what the economic data says about the economy.

That’s Jack Bowman with Bowman Capital Management on Seeking Alpha.

One of the big major players in consumer sentiment, which is at an all time low and largely driven by this feeling that the economy is not as good as the data says it is.

The most recent data shows The Consumer Price Index rose 2.7% Y/Y in November.

Although it's very regionally dependent, right? Where I live in southern California our Y/Y inflation was 4.5% but someone in Dallas saw a 1.1%, way far off.

According to the latest data, December consumer sentiment was revised down to 52.9 from the University of Michigan’s initial estimate of 53.3, though it improved from 51.0 in November.

Gains in sentiment were concentrated among lower-income consumers, while sentiment among higher-income consumers remained steady. Meanwhile, year-ahead inflation expectations declined for a fourth straight month to 4.2%—the lowest level in 11 months—though still above the 3.3% recorded in January.

So, what could potentially turn things around?

One of the things I think will help is time, unfortunately.

Number two is the one that you can probably count on being on everyone’s list.

It has to be tariffs and liberation day was the most watched day in market history for the recent years.

April 2, 2025

So, the rates we saw that day no longer matter. That famous image of President Trump with the big board with all the numbers on it, none of those are accurate to what it is now.

But Jack admits sometimes things don’t play out the way you expect them to.

Some of these were geopolitically useful but some of these I think were taken at face value right away. And I’m guilty of that too. When those numbers came out I said, “Oh no this is going to be really bad.” And since then I've tapered back a lot of my expectations about how bad it's actually going to be.

It’s December, where are we now? Well this act is still going…

The only thing I think that could be unpredictable for 2026 is how the China situation is going to unfold. We’re currently in a trade truce for the next year so most of 2026 will be spent on negotiating what that end result will be and I have no idea where that’s going to go.

If you’re searching for the latest news and analysis on tariffs. Just put the word tariffs in the search bar on Seeking Alpha and it comes right up.

The biggest one to me was AI capex spending.

Big tech companies including Alphabet (GOOG, GOOGL), Amazon (AMZN), Microsoft (MSFT), and Meta (META) are investing billions in AI infrastructure as the race to lead in AI accelerates.

I kind of make this joke that 2025 was the year where we went from covering the planet in solar panels to covering the planet in data centers. We made the switch really fast.

Let’s consider a few of these: Meta (META) expects 2025 capital expenditures, including principal payments on finance leases, to be in the range of $70B to $72B, increased from its prior outlook of $66B to $72B.

Meta said it will "spend aggressively" next year, warning its capital expenditures will be "notably larger" in 2026 and total expenses will grow at a "significantly faster percentage rate."

Alphabet (GOOG) (GOOGL) raised its forecast for capital expenditure for 2025 and 2026.

"We're continuing to invest aggressively due to the demand we're experiencing from Cloud customers as well as the growth opportunities we see across the company. We now expect CapEx to be in the range of $91 billion to $93 billion in 2025, up from our previous estimate of $85 billion," said Alphabet's Senior Vice President and CFO Anat Ashkenazi during the third quarter earnings call.

Amazon (AMZN) said its cash CapEx was $34.2B in the third quarter and the company has spent $89.9B so far this year. The e-commerce giant noted that it will continue to make significant investments, especially in AI.

So I’ve been happy with how much is being spent because it means we’re getting to that phase where we don’t have to spend as much anymore sooner.

Meta Platforms (META) has a Quant rating of HOLD on Seeking Alpha. Alphabet (GOOG) (GOOGL) has the same rating though Seeking Alpha analysts say it’s a buy and Wall Street says it’s a strong buy. Amazon’s (AMZN) Quant rating on Seeking Alpha is Strong Buy.

Enough about 2025, what about next year?

I’m calling that I think the S&P is gonna rise another 10-15%.

But why?

I mostly give that bullish take because I don't see a lot of the tailwinds behind technology stocks like their big spending. The fact that most of them are insulated and immune from tariffs, at least the big 10 companies that make up half of the S&P. And they seem to continuously be expanding their profit margins even as they get into what I assume will be a very low margin business, AI.

So there you have it, a broad look at 2025 and a prediction for 2026.

By the way, if you’re interested in the Top Stocks of 2026. I’ll leave a link so that you can register for our webinar coming up on the 6th of January with our VP of Quantitative Strategy Steven Cress. This is a hot ticket every year.

No special hours for the market today.

Thanks for listening to part 1 of our year-in-review. I’ll have part 2 on New Year's Eve. It’s all about the headlines that you were most interested in.

Enjoy your weekend. I’ll see you back here on Monday.

data.title: Rumble: A Post-Acquisition Look
data.description:
Shares of video-sharing platform and cloud services provider Rumble Inc. (RUM) are lower since news of its proposed acquisition of Northern Data AG (NDTAF) broke on November 11, 2025. A major shareholder in both concerns, stablecoin behemoth Tether announced that it will commit $100 million to advertising and $150 million to data center leasing over the ensuing two years. With its video sharing suffering from declining viewership and its post-Northern close valuation near seven times FY26 revenue, the recent Tether buying did at least merit a deeper dive. An analysis follows below.

Company Overview:

Rumble Inc. is a Longboat Key, Florida-based video-sharing platform and cloud services provider, connecting content creators with brands, publishers, and their subscribers. The company brands itself as a free-speech alternative to Google’s (GOOG) (GOOGL) YouTube, having 47 million average global monthly active users (MAU) in 3Q25, while its budding infrastructure-as-a-service and impending GPU-as-a-service businesses will be supported by nine data centers upon the closing of its acquisition of Northern Data Group. Rumble was founded in 2013 and went public in 2022 when it reverse merged into blank check concern CF Acquisition Corp. VI, with its first trade transacted at $12.44 a share. Its stock trades around seven bucks a share, translating to a market cap of just under $2.4 billion.

The company is capitalized by three classes of stock. The 215.4 million shares of publicly traded Class A stock bestow economic interest and one vote per share. The 123.7 million shares of privately held Class C stock confer no economic interest, one vote per share, and convertibility into Class A shares. The 95.8 million shares of privately held Class D shares bestow no economic interest, 11.2663 votes per share, and convertibility into Class A shares. Owing to this arrangement, Founder, Chairman & CEO Chris Pavlosvski controls 83% of his company’s voting power. When its merger with Northern Data closes, the Class A count will increase by ~189.9 million shares.

Business Models

Rumble currently generates revenue from two sources: Audience Monetization (AM) and Other Initiatives (OI).

AM consists of advertising fees, subscription fees primarily from Rumble Premium, content licensed by third parties, pay-per-view, and platform hosting fees. It was responsible for revenue of $62.8 million in the first nine months of 2025 (YTD25), representing 85% of the total and a 10% increase over YTD24. Approximately two-thirds of the platform’s users are male, with approximately the same percentage of total viewership from the U.S.

OI includes digital ads on the company’s network of third-party publisher websites, as well as fees from cloud hosting services—most notably Trump Media & Technology’s Truth Social. It accounted for YTD25 revenue of $10.8 million, representing 15% of total revenue and a 30% increase over YTD25.

Pivot Towards AI Infrastructure

Popular among conservative podcasters and their followers, Rumble’s video-sharing platform has failed to generate a profit. With a history of tough sledding with advertisers due to its free speech stance and seeing its average global MAU peak at ~80 million in 4Q22, the company generated Adj. EBITDA of negative $115.3 million on revenue of $81.0 million in FY23 and Adj. EBITDA of negative $92.1 million on revenue of $95.5 million in FY24.

With its stock spending most of the 4Q23-4Q24 time frame languishing between $5 and $7 per share, Rumble’s stock soared on news of a $775 million investment from stablecoin provider Tether in December 2024. The company stated that $250 million would be earmarked for growth initiatives, with the balance going to purchase 70 million shares at $7.50 in a tender offering—essentially rotating shareholders. In addition to the financing, the deal included plans for advertising, cloud, and crypto payment solutions, with shares of RUM surging 126% to $16.27 over the subsequent three trading sessions. Approximately three months after that transaction, Rumble’s stock was trading below $7.50, with its average global MAU dropping to the aforementioned 47 million in 3Q25, its lowest level since 2Q22—albeit under a slightly different methodology—as competitors such as YouTube have become less restrictive regarding content publication.

That said, the company’s relationship with deep-pocketed and highly profitable Tether intensified when it entered into an agreement to acquire German AI data center concern Northern Data Group for 2.0281 shares of RUM for every share of NDTAF, valuing the deal (based on Rumble’s December 24th, 2025 close) at $1.4 billion, which is approximately equal to Northern Data’s infrastructure investment. The acquiree is expected to generate revenue of ~$42 million in 4Q25.

The transaction brings Rumble 22,400 GPUs housed across nine data centers and a meaningful presence in Europe. Tether is a major shareholder in Northern Data and essentially brokered the deal, which was announced on November 10, 2025, and is expected to close in 2Q26. Furthermore, the stablecoin leader pledged $150 million in GPU usage and $100 million of advertising on the video-sharing platform—both over the ensuing two years—with the latter intended to drive usage and engagement of Rumble Wallet, which was launched in 3Q25.

Unlike the initial Tether investment from nearly 11 months prior, this news failed to engender the same enthusiasm, with shares of RUM enjoying a short-lived rally of 11% to $6.56 in the subsequent trading session.

3Q25 Financials

In addition to the significant dilution of the impending deal—essentially a 69.6% Rumble, 30.4% Northern Data split post-close—part of the reason for the market’s skepticism was the continuing stagnation of its primary video-sharing service business, the 3Q25 results of which were announced on the same day as the Northern Data deal. Rumble posted a loss of $0.06 a share (GAAP) and Adj. EBITDA of negative $15.1 million on revenue of $24.8 million versus a loss of $0.15 a share (GAAP) and Adj. EBITDA of negative $23.5 million on revenue of $25.1 million in 3Q24. The biggest bright spot was average revenue per user increased 7% sequentially to $0.45, which almost entirely offset the 8% sequential drop in MAU to 47 million.

With the focus on Northern Data and Tether, management provided little commentary on its core business.

Balance Sheet & Analyst Commentary:

Thanks in large measure to Tether, Rumble’s balance sheet remains pristine despite the continuing losses, featuring cash and equivalents of $270 million and bitcoin holdings of $24 million as of September 30, 2025.

The company does not enjoy much sponsorship from the Street, which can be seen above. On average, they expect Rumble to lose $0.29 per share (GAAP) on revenue of $100 million in FY25, followed by a loss of $0.19 per share (GAAP) on revenue of $452 million in FY26.

One entity that has not lost enthusiasm for the company is Tether, which purchased 1.06 million shares of RUM on November 19-21, 2025, upping its stake to 104.4 million shares and its ownership interest to 24%.

Verdict:

It would appear that Rumble’s sponsor, Tether, is trying to will it to victory, providing it with $125 million in annual anchor tenant and advertising revenue over the next two years until it hooks another major tenant for its future data centers. And to be fair, after the Northern Data merger closes, the just under $4 billion market cap will look relatively cheap compared to hyperscaler Nebius (NBIS), with its seven data centers, ~30,000 GPUs, and ~$21 billion market cap. The difference is that Nebius’ mission has always been AI infrastructure, and it is expected to generate $3.1 billion from its business in FY26 with a YE26 annual run rate of $8 billion, having sold out its capacity and inking a $3 billion deal with Meta Platforms (META) on November 11, 2025. By contrast, Northern Data was a bitcoin miner that is selling that part of its operation to reinvest the proceeds (reportedly $200 million) into high-performance computing and AI infrastructure. In other words, it is relatively unestablished—besides for its relationship with Tether.

That said, Tether is deep-pocketed, but post-close this has the appearance of a company with a best-case FY26 pro forma revenue of $500 million, approximately a quarter to a fifth of which is unprofitable, and a just south of $4 billion market cap. Trading at nearly eight times next year’s pro forma revenue post-close—in a very optimistic scenario—Rumble is too rich, unless it can attract some major tenants. In the meantime, no investment is recommended.

data.title: Invesco Rochester Limited Term New York Municipal Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Key takeaways

1 Fund performance

Invesco Rochester® Limited Term New York Municipal Fund (IRNYX, IRNFX, IRNYY) Class A shares at net asset value (NAV) outperformed its style-specific index, the S&P Municipal New York Investment Grade 4-7 Years Bond Index.

2 Seeking attractive opportunities through collaborative management

Invesco Municipal Bond team uses a collaborative management approach. Relying on our size and experience, we seek to identify the best opportunities to achieve potentially better outcomes for shareholders.

3 Analysis focused on creditworthiness

Our team uses a bottom-up fundamental credit process focused on creditworthiness of individual issuers with an overlay of macroeconomic factors to capitalize on market inefficiencies. Our process has been time tested over full market cycles.

Manager perspective and outlook

  • In the third quarter, investment grade, high yield and taxable municipals delivered positive returns of 2.87%, 1.58% and 2.66%, respectively. 1 The municipal market saw mixed performance: July and August continued the previous quarter's underperformance, but September brought strong performance in long duration municipals as US Treasuries rallied and municipal bond fund flows increased.
  • New municipal bond issuance reached $153 billion in the third quarter, bringing the year-to-date total to $437 billion. This is 13% higher than the same period in 2024 and significantly above previous years. 1
  • Municipal fund net flows were a highlight, totaling about $16.2 billion for the quarter and $31.8 billion year to date. 2
  • In September, the US Federal Reserve (Fed) made its first rate cut of the year, lowering the federal funds rate by 0.25%. The Fed cited increased downside risks to employment and reiterated its commitment to balancing its dual mandate of maximum employment and a 2% inflation target. Future policy decisions will depend on incoming data, the evolving outlook and the balance of risks. 3
  • We believe state and municipal budgets remain healthy. Although the pace of credit rating upgrades has slowed, credit fundamentals still appear strong and upgrades have outpaced downgrades so far this year. 4
  • The quarter ended with the threat of a government shutdown as Congress struggled to pass funding bills needed to keep federal agencies operating in the fiscal year beginning October 1st. 1
  • During periods of policy uncertainty, we rely on our experienced credit research team to identify and capitalize on marketplace dislocations in order to deliver value to shareholders.
  • Looking ahead, we see opportunities in the municipal bond market. With potential for further Fed rate cuts and steady new municipal issuance, we believe high absolute yields and strong fundamentals make municipals an appealing option.

Portfolio positioning

Yield Curve and Duration Positioning: Overall, we maintain a constructive outlook for duration and are looking to selectively add longer duration securities to the fund when appropriate and according to prospectus guidelines. We maintain an overweight in the shorter maturity segment of the yield curve given the fund's short duration mandate.

Sector allocations: We maintain an overweight position in revenue bonds relative to state and local general obligation bonds. Within the revenue bond segment, the fund's largest exposures are in the dedicated tax, higher education and tobacco settlement sectors.

Credit Conditions: We maintain a positive view on fundamentals in the municipal market as upgrades of credit ratings have continued to outpace downgrades. Various sectors have been under pressure due to uncertainty surrounding federal funding and tariff policy, as well as higher labor costs. We are monitoring these sectors closely and continue to rely on our extensive bottom-up research capabilities to find pockets of opportunity.

Performance highlights

The third quarter had a volatile start as record levels of new issuance and uncertainty related to fiscal and monetary policies weighed on municipal valuations. Momentum shifted in late August as labor market weakness fueled expectations that the Fed would resume monetary easing. Municipals rallied strongly through September and ended the quarter with positive returns. As expected, the Fed cut the federal funds rates by 0.25% in September and in its comments on future actions indicated a stance favoring easier monetary policy. Yields fell across the municipal yield curve, with yields on two- and five-year bonds down 0.35% and 0.41%, respectively. 5 Higher quality municipal credits generally outperformed for the quarter.

Contributors to performance

Contributors to relative return this quarter included the following:

The fund's overweight in bonds with durations between 10 and 15 years contributed to relative performance. Security selection among bonds rated AA and higher added to relative performance, as did security selection in the dedicated tax sector.

data.title: Harbor Scientific Alpha High-Yield ETF Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
The backdrop of continuing U.S. labor market weakness has been increasingly reflected in consumer sentiment surveys and upward trends in credit delinquencies. - BlueCove Limited

Market in Review

Risk markets remained firm during the third quarter, with U.S. equities gaining 8% and U.S. high yield up 2%. Volatility was muted despite ongoing geopolitical risk in the Middle East and Ukraine. Risk markets were buoyed by expectations for rate cuts by the U.S. Federal Reserve, as concerns shifted from inflation to an increasingly weak labor market. Though spread dispersion in high-yield markets was muted on average during the quarter, selective opportunities in security selection emerged.

Portfolio Performance

During the third quarter, the Harbor Scientific Alpha High Yield ETF ("ETF") returned 2.70% (NAV), outperforming its benchmark, the ICE BofA US High Yield Index (H0A0), which returned 2.40%. The outperformance was driven by security selection across several sectors.

Overweights and Underweights

The ETF seeks to be broadly neutral in duration and yield curve versus its benchmark. During the quarter, the largest sector positions were an overweight, in risk terms, to Consumer Cyclical companies and an underweight to Communications. In aggregate, sector positioning detracted 1 basis point from performance versus the benchmark. The ETF was underweight lower-quality high yield, with a varying allocation to higher-quality high yield, reflecting the current opportunity set. We seek to invest only in index-eligible securities and U.S. dollar securities, and we do not emphasize country selection versus the benchmark.

Outlook

The backdrop of continuing U.S. labor market weakness has been increasingly reflected in consumer sentiment surveys and upward trends in credit delinquencies. We believe these factors underpin concerns that expensive risky asset valuations belie macroeconomic fundamentals. BlueCove's U.S. Economic Regime Indicator shifted from "Contraction" to "Slowdown" in August, due to diminished risk of a trade war tipping the economy into recession. However, considering the labor market, our indicator remains close to the threshold that flags elevated recessionary risk. We see default incidence remaining above median and view this, alongside the fragile economic context, as a positive backdrop for the ETF's security-selection emphasis.

data.title: Gabelli Media Mogul Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Strategy Overview

The Gabelli Media Mogul Fund offers the opportunity to invest primarily in companies in which Dr. John Malone has a direct or indirect ownership stake.

Investment Scorecard

The performance of most John Malone-related stocks continued to rebound in the third quarter. Warner Bros. Discovery (10.3% of net assets as of September 30, 2025; +70%) was by far the largest contributor to returns after speculation that Paramount Skydance was interested in acquiring the entire company prior to its planned spin-off of its cable networks. Such reports remain unconfirmed at this writing, though we have long believed consolidation was likely. Live entertainment companies such as Liberty Live (LLYVA, LLYVK) (8.9%; +19%) and Sphere Entertainment (SPHR) (2.7%; +49%) continued to perform well despite (or perhaps because of) a stressed consumer. Detractors from performance primarily included U.S. broadband providers Charter Communications (CHTR) (0.9%; -33%) and its 26% owner Liberty Broadband (K: 4.3%; -31%) and Comcast Corp. (2.5%; -11%), each of which face increased competition from fiber and wireless. During the quarter, Liberty Broadband completed the spin-off of its converged Alaskan telecommunications unit GCI Liberty (GLIBA) (2.0%; +20%) which performed well in its debut.

Let's Talk Stocks

Atlanta Braves Holding's (13.2% of net assets as of September 30, 2025) (BATRK - $41.59 - NASDAQ) primary assets are the Atlanta Braves baseball club and the mixed-use real estate development known as "The Battery" surrounding Truist Park. The Braves, founded in 1871, are the oldest continuously operating professional sports franchise in U.S. with fans across the Southeast U.S. The team has recently reclaimed much of its prior success and were 2021 World Champions. Long term, team values should be supported by growing media revenue and the growth of recently legalized sports betting. Formerly a tracker stock of Liberty Media Corp., in July 2023 Liberty split-off the Braves as an asset-backed company, which should facilitate an eventual sale.

Liberty Broadband's (4.3%) (LBRDK - $63.54 - NASDAQ) principal asset is a 26% interest in Charter Communications, the second largest cable company in the United States. Liberty Broadband is the product of decades of financial engineering by cable pioneer Dr. John Malone, who effectively controls the company. Charter successfully integrated the acquisitions of Time Warner Cable and Bright House and recently announced the acquisition of Cox Communications, all with the intent of capitalizing on its broadband infrastructure and wireless bundle during a time of increasing internet usage. In keeping with the precedent of other Liberty investments (e.g., DirecTV), in November 2024 Liberty Broadband announced it would merger with Charter Communications in a transaction that should close coincident with the Cox transaction.

Warner Bros. Discovery Inc. (10.3%) (WBD - $19.53 - NASDAQ) is the product of the April 2022 merger of Warner Bros. and Discovery Communications. Warner Bros. is one of Hollywood's oldest and most prolific studios and was once the crown jewel of Time Warner alongside HBO and the Turner Networks. AT&T (T) purchased Time Warner in 2018. Discovery began as a non-fiction cable network with a significant stake owned by John Malone's Liberty Media. The company has successfully reduced the combined company's cost base and leverage and shepherded its Max streaming service to profitability. In June 2025, WBD announced that it would separate its studio and streaming business from its linear networks business sometime in 2026; more recently, the company has been speculated as a takeover target for Paramount Skydance.

data.title: GS Enhanced Income, Short Duration Bond And Short Duration Government Funds Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Review

Q3 2025 proved to be a generally favorable quarter for global financial markets, with bonds experiencing a rally. This positive momentum was largely underpinned by the continued resilience of global growth, as the anticipated negative impact of tariffs was less severe than initially feared at the close of Q2. A crucial development was the emergence of growing signs of weakness in the US labor market, which ultimately prompted the Federal Reserve to implement a rate cut in September. A significant catalyst was the July US jobs report, released in early August, which was considerably weaker than expected and included substantial downward revisions (-258k) for May and June, the largest monthly downward revisions since May 2020.

Enhanced Income Fund

Attribution: The Fund outperformed the benchmark over the third quarter of 2025 on a gross and net basis.

Duration: The portfolio’s duration strategy detracted from returns over the quarter, largely driven by our overweight exposure to the 2-year node of the USD curve, particularly in July, where it sold off by 24bps. July’s sell-off was driven by persistent inflation, new tariffs, and robust economic data, alongside the Fed's hawkish stance. This reversed in August, as a softer US jobs report spurred a rally in 1- and 2-year nodes, increasing September rate cut expectations. The Federal Reserve delivered a 25bps rate cut in September, citing employment risks. However, persistent inflation and an upward revision to Q2 GDP (to 3.8%) tempered market expectations for further cuts, recalibrating future policy. The Fed delivered its first rate cut of 2025 in September, a 25bp reduction that lowered the target range for the federal funds rate to 4.00-4.25%. The Fed also indicated further rate cuts, with the median dot plot, or Summary of Economic Projections, projecting an additional 50bps in cuts by the end of 2025.

Fund Performance (%)

data.title: Gold Breaks A 40-Year Correlation - Does This Signal A Problem For S&P 500 Forward Returns?
data.description:
It’s difficult to look at this TrendSpider chart and not be be bullish gold, silver and platinum.

Although this blog missed the move in gold (and never traded silver or platinum), having followed the precious metal since the late 1970s when Paul Volcker was Fed Chairman, the fascinating aspect to this latest price move in gold is that it’s occurring with positive correlation to the S&P 500, as opposed to the negative correlation that gold had with equities for the last 40 years.

Gold bullion (gold bars) peaked around $800 per ounce in the 1980, just as Paul Volcker was maxing out his Fed fund rate increases near 20% in the spring of 1980 - the Fed funds rate peaked at 20% and the 30-year Treasury yield peaked around 15% at the same time - and then gold began to slide through the secular bull market for the S&P 500 from August 1982 to March 2000.

Gold bullion (again) began to bottom in late 1999, around $200 an ounce, and really took off again as the technology and growth stocks resulted in the S&P 500 peaking in March 2000, and then gained steam for the rest of the decade from 2000 through 2010. The problem was - for investors - that you were forced to buy gold-related equities like miners or common stocks for clients, like Newmont Mining (NEM), whose revenue, earnings and cash flow at the time were driven primarily by gold mining. (Per Morningstar, Newmont Mining is the world’s largest gold miner.)

That was the case until SPDR (S&P Depository Receipts) created the GLD ETF that was launched in January ’05, which promptly became one of the biggest ETFs in the capital markets by the late 2000s. Obviously, the GLD ETF allowed investors to buy exposure to gold in a traditional equity instrument like an ETF without having to buy an operating company whose business was tied to gold mining.

This GLD monthly chart shows GLD peaking at $185 in the month of September 2011, which is 30 months after the S&P 500 bottomed from its historic bear market on March 9th, 2009.

Annual Returns of Gold vs. the S&P 500

This YCharts graphic shows the annual returns of the GLD ETF from its first month of trading through its all-time high month in September 2011. Even with the S&P 500 bottoming in March 2009, the performance disparity is considerable.

Note the magnitude of GLD’s outperformance relative to the S&P 500's total return from 2005 through September 2011.

Now, let’s look at the GLD vs. the S&P 500 annual returns from September 2011 through October ’23, when GLD broke above the September 2011 high:

The negative correlation should be obvious to readers given the returns from when the S&P 500 was in a bear market, versus the ensuing period when the S&P 500 was in a fresh, new bull market.

Is gold telling investors something today about the prospective returns for the S&P 500? Is the negative correlation between the two asset classes which existed for roughly 40 years (and maybe longer than that) now completely broken, and are the two asset classes now positively correlated?

Summary / conclusion

While this blog is cautious on prospective S&P 500 and Nasdaq returns going forward, this blog post today is really a way to show readers that correlations between asset classes do matter - and that they also can change over time too.

The fact that the GLD didn’t peak in September 2011, until after 30 months after the S&P 500 bottomed, also tells me that there can be lags in the correlation. The bottom for gold bullion in late 1999 and the peak for the S&P 500 and Nasdaq in March 2000 was much tighter than the time lag between the GLD peak in September 2011 and the bottom for the S&P 500.

Is gold signaling an issue for the S&P 500 and the Nasdaq again? It’s possible, although GLD’s breakout in October 2023 has now occurred even as the S&P 500 made new highs in late 2025.

Bitcoin - once referred to as digital gold - does present an interesting variable to this whole debate. Bitcoin seems to have weakened in price while the rally in gold grows stronger, even though they can be thought of as similar asset classes, since neither are operating entities nor do they generate any cash flow or free cash flow.

From 1980 to 2000, and then from 2010 to October ’23 gives readers a feel for how long the “gold versus S&P 500” performance dispersion can last.

Readers should take away from all this that - either the rally in gold stalls here pretty soon as the S&P 500 hits new all-time highs, or the S&P 500’s prospective returns start to diminish given the traditional correlation history between gold and S&P 500 returns.

None of this is advice or a recommendation, but only an opinion. Past performance is no guarantee of future results. Clients should gauge their own comfort with portfolio volatility and act accordingly.

Thanks for reading.

data.title: QDVO: Tech-Heavy Covered Call ETF With Upside And Strong Income
data.description:
Artificial intelligence has been a major theme for investors ever since OpenAI’s ChatGPT captured the imaginations of users worldwide in 2022. Now, three years later, the advent of ChatGPT marked an important inflection point that resulted in explosive growth in market caps, revenues, and CapEx spending in the technology sector. According to a market intelligence firm, Gardner, Data Center-related CapEx spending will continue to grow in 2026, which should help the Amplify CWP Growth & Income ETF (QDVO) achieve strong net asset value returns. The QDVO is an actively-managed covered call ETF with a heavy tilt towards large-cap growth equities, and the fund has achieved an impressive return record, given its short operating history.

Overview of the DQVO

The Amplify CWP Growth & Income ETF is a covered call ETF that pursues two investment objectives at the same time. The primary investment objective is to generate high recurring dividend income from writing call options on fast-growing U.S. technology companies with strong earnings profiles. The secondary investment objective is to create long-term NAV upside and allow investors to participate in the growth of the share prices of major U.S. technology companies, especially the Magnificent 7 companies, all of which have material AI exposure.

While the official benchmark for the Amplify CWP Growth & Income ETF is the S&P 500 Growth Index, the ETF is heavily focused on companies listed on the Nasdaq. The core function of the Amplify CWP Growth & Income ETF is, as I explained above, to increase investors monthly cash flow through the application of an option overlay strategy. To do this, the ETF purchases shares in major companies like Nvidia (NVDA), Meta Platforms (META), and Tesla (TSLA), to name a few examples, and writes call options on these names. The option income is passed through to investors of the ETF, which makes the QDVO an attractive vehicle for investors that want to monetize a long position in the Magnificent 7 companies.

The Amplify CWP Growth & Income ETF currently has approximately $529M in assets under management, which compares to $32.6B for the JPMorgan Nasdaq Equity Premium Income ETF (JEPQ), a heavyweight Nasdaq-focused covered call ETF, and $7.3B for the NEOS NASDAQ-100(R) High Income ETF (QQQI), which I previously singled out as a very promising investment option for investors that want to combine tech exposure with a 13% yield at the time: Strong Outperformance Potential.

While the Amplify CWP Growth & Income ETF is the smallest ETF here, as well as the one with the shortest performance record—the ETF was incepted only on August 22, 2024—it has a very favorable fund flow picture. This year, the ETF attracted almost half a billion dollars in net fund inflows, highlighting strong market interest in high-yielding, monthly-paying covered call ETFs with a tech bent.

The biggest investment position for the QDVO is Nvidia, with an investment share of 10.8%, followed by Apple (AAPL), with a share of 10.3%, and Microsoft (MSFT), with a share of 8.2%. All the Magnificent 7 stocks are included in the ETF’s investment portfolio plus AMD (AMD).

AMD and other chip companies, especially Nvidia and Broadcom, are seeing massive demand for their GPUs and AI accelerators following break-through achievements in the field of artificial intelligence in the last several years. According to Gartner, Data Centers are going to continue to be growth drivers for CapEx spending next year, which means AI-themed investments have a strong chance of performing well in 2026. Nvidia came to the same conclusion and projected that the Data Center market could be worth between $3 trillion and $4 trillion by the end of the decade.

The biggest sector by far for the Amplify CWP Growth & Income is Information Technology, representing a massive 41.1% of all investments in the ETF’s portfolio. The top ten holdings in the Amplify CWP Growth & Income represented approximately 62% of all investments, which is the highest investment percentage when compared against JEPQ and QQQI. The Amplify CWP Growth & Income also has the highest Nvidia rating (~11%) and has the most aggressive weighting of the Magnificent 7 stocks (~51%).

The QDVO would therefore be an attractive investment product for investors that 1) Have a favorable long-term outlook on the tech industry and AI especially, 2) Want to monetize a long position and create monthly cash flow through covered call strategies, and 3) Like the aggressive positioning of the ETF in terms of its tech-focused top ten holdings.

Valuation comparison: QDVO vs. JEPQ vs. QQQI

The QDVO has a very solid return performance so far, despite being around only for a little more than one year. Since inception, the Amplify CWP Growth & Income has achieved a return on net asset value of 25.18%, compared to 20.80% for QQQI and 16.57% for the JEPQ. The QDVO has therefore beaten the net asset value performance of its ETF rivals and is due to an aggressive overweighting of Magnificent 7 stocks that did well during the last year.

Risks with QDVO

There are a number of risks for ETFs in general as well as for QDVO specifically. A general risk for covered call ETFs is 'capped upside.' Because covered call funds write call options that are likely to get exercised if the stock price rises sharply, they have limited upside potential.

The Amplify CWP Growth & Income also has specific risks, such as its high-tech concentration as well as the fact that the ETF has only been around for a little more than a year. Therefore, the ETF has not proven itself over a longer period of time and has, therefore, a less reliable performance sheet than some of its bigger ETF rivals.

Final thoughts

The Amplify CWP Growth & Income is a potentially attractive investment choice for investors that want to monetize a long position in technology companies, and especially Magnificent 7 stocks, and use this long position as a lever for an option overlay strategy to generate recurring monthly dividend income. This dividend income currently calculates to an 11% yield, and investors get above-average exposure to the world’s leading technology companies.

The Amplify CWP Growth & Income has a solid performance history so far and has a more aggressively structured investment portfolio than its rivals, meaning the QDVO has an especially strong concentration in fast-growing Magnificent 7 companies. Since Gartner is projecting continued upside in Data Center CapEx spending in 2026, a viewpoint that has been supported by Nvidia’s projection about Data Center market expansion until the end of the decade, I believe the QDVO is a speculative buy for investors that want to build strategic exposure to the tech sector.

data.title: Money Supply Grows For A 25th Consecutive Month
data.description:
Money Supply is a very important indicator. It helps show how tight or loose current monetary conditions are regardless of what the Fed is doing with interest rates. Even if the Fed is tight, if Money Supply is increasing, it has an inflationary effect.

One key metric shown below is the “Wenzel” 13-week annualized money supply figure. It was made popular by the late Robert Wenzel, who tracked the metric weekly as an indicator for where the economy might be headed. In 2020, the Fed started reporting the data monthly instead of weekly. It should also be noted that Money Supply data can be heavily revised in future months.

Recent Trends

Seasonally Adjusted Money Supply has been growing on a consistent monthly basis since November 2023 (25 straight months). The latest month (November) showed a modest increase of $24B. While this is the smallest growth since December of last year, it is still positive.

Figure 1: MoM M2 Change (Seasonally Adjusted)

The increase was 1.3% annualized, which is well below the 4.3% over the past year.

Figure 2: M2 Growth Rates

November average is typically around 4.5%, which puts the latest month well below average.

Figure 3: Average Monthly Growth Rates

Non-seasonally adjusted shows positive growth for the months of November and December (note: this data is ahead of the seasonally adjusted data). The last two months have been the strongest two months since June.

Figure 4: MoM M2 Change (Non-Seasonally Adjusted)

The weekly data shows what happened. There was a decent pop in the most recent week of $122B for the week of Dec 1st. Keep in mind, this is unadjusted, which is why it does not match Figure 1.

Figure 5: WoW M2 Change

The “Wenzel” 13-week Money Supply

The late Robert Wenzel of Economic Policy Journal used a modified calculation to track Money Supply. He used a trailing 13-week average growth rate annualized as defined in his book The Fed Flunks. He specifically used the weekly data that was not seasonally adjusted. His analogy was that in order to know what to wear outside, he wants to know the current weather, not temperatures that have been averaged throughout the year.

The objective of the 13-week average is to smooth some of the choppy data without bringing in too much history that could blind someone from seeing what’s in front of them. The 13-week average growth rate can be seen in the table below.

Growth was accelerating for 13 straight weeks but has slowed a touch to 5.08%. After a brief 3-week deceleration, there has been growth the last 2 weeks, which has put it back above 5%.

Figure 6: WoW Trailing 13-week Average Money Supply Growth

The plot below shows how this year compares with previous years. This year started at about the average point for the last 10 years and has stayed in that range the entire year. Growth is typically flat to up for the last two months of the year. It appears this year should fit in the same line. It wouldn’t be surprising to see a pop in the last few weeks of the year, which will be reported next month.

Figure 7: Yearly 13-week Overlay

Inflation and Money Supply

The chart below shows the history of inflation, Money Supply, and Fed Funds. As shown, in 1970 inflation worked with a ~2-year lag compared to Money Supply. Money Supply slowed dramatically in 2023 and 2024 but has been moving back up. Inflation has also been stickier than the Fed would like, but unfortunately, they cannot do much given the large debt load of the US Government and Corporations. Despite inflation staying elevated, even moving up in the recent period, the Fed has no choice but to continue lowering rates.

The inflation rate is now only 1.2% below the fed funds rate. That is not a big enough gap to slow inflation, especially when the money supply is still expanding.

Figure 8: YoY M2 Change with CPI and Fed Funds

Historical Perspective

The charts below are designed to put the current trends into historical perspective. The orange bars represent annualized percentage change rather than raw dollar amount.

Figure 9: M2 with Growth Rate

Below shows the 13-week annualized average over history. This chart overlays the log return of the S&P. Mr. Wenzel proposed that large drops in Money Supply could be a sign of stock market pullbacks. His theory, derived from Murray Rothbard, states that when the market experiences a shrinking growth rate of Money Supply (or even negative), it can create liquidity issues in the stock market, leading to a sell-off.

While not a perfect predictive tool, many of the dips in Money Supply precede market dips. Specifically, the major dips in 2002 and 2008 from +10% down to 0%. 2022 was highly correlated with a fall in Money Supply and the rebound has corresponded with the big stock market move we saw in 2023 and into 2024. The slowing growth in 2025 did correlate with the market drop in March/April, but that was clearly more headline driven. With money growth continuing to climb, it seems to be providing enough fuel to keep pushing the market higher. The current November market correction looks like it will likely be shallow since the money supply remains elevated

Please note the chart only shows market data through Dec 1st to align with available M2 data.

Figure 10: 13-week M2 Annualized and S&P 500

One other consideration is the reverse repo market at the Fed. This is a tool that allows financial institutions to swap cash for instruments on the Fed balance sheet.

Reverse repos peaked at $2.55T on Dec 30, 2022. Money gushed out from March 2023 to May 2024. The outflows have taken another leg down as the remaining balances have been drained. The balance is now at $5.9B (as of Dec. 23).

Figure 11: Fed Reverse Repurchase Agreements

Wrapping Up

The Fed is continuing to cut rates while inflation is moving up. With $38.5T in debt (and growing extremely fast), they have no choice. To keep the debt load sustainable, rates have to come down. Gold at $4,500 is screaming from the mountaintops that this is not a good idea. Gold does need some time to cool off, but there is nothing in the fundamentals that tells you the gold bull market is over. If anything, it is still in the early innings. Silver has lagged for years and is finally catching up. It also needs to take a much-needed breather, but dips should be considered buying opportunities.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.
